content.body: On this, I think the following might be interesting: I few months ago I added use counters to see if people did actually change the type of <button> and <input>: https://chromium-review.googlesource.com/c/chromium/src/+/7624034. Changing type while a <button> is connected to the DOM -> ~6% vs disconnected -> ~25% of page loads. Changing type while an <input> is connected to the DOM -> ~1% vs disconnected ~70%. This + having a few people independently bring up not following the <input type> pattern and this article https://meowni.ca/posts/a-story-about-input/, informed our decision to not have dynamic changes until we hear a valid/real use case for it (which I've asked for but have not heard any yet).

I must say that I'm not opposed to the idea of dynamic behaviors, there's certainly some design/implementation issues I'd be worried about though. This is more just explaining why I didn't choose that direction in this public iteration. I'm certainly open to feedback as to why dynamic is preferred and happy to explore/prototype that.
I'm not sure statistics about where `type` is mutated tell us much. You'd also need to know over what period of time it is mutated to know if it is relevant or not.
content.formatted_body: I'm not sure statistics about where type is mutated tell us much. You'd also need to know over what period of time it is mutated to know if it is relevant or not.

content.body: Overall I'm not really convinced that this is sufficient to break with prior established patterns.

Hey folks, on May 18th https://linegoup.lol shows a big drop in passing tests for almost all engines. Looking at individual browser results, https://wpt.fyi/results/wasm/core?label=canary&from=2026-05-18T06%3A00%3A00.000Z&to=2026-05-18T20%3A00%3A00.000Z&product=chrome points to a big drop in WASM test scores. The only commit that appears to be in this timeframe is https://github.com/web-platform-tests/wpt/commit/49981e3a30d6ad4fccceae8a0e834e043d2f048a I don't know whether this could cause this big drop, but maybe somebody with WASM knowledge knows what's going on here? It's a scheduled sync (https://github.com/web-platform-tests/wpt/pull/59934), but I don't know what the source is (the PR doesn't list that in its description). So I also don't know where to report this finding.

Jake Archibald Alice (🇳🇱) smaug maybe we could organize a small breakout meeting next week to go over https://github.com/WICG/webcomponents/issues/1098? Seems straightforward since we're all in Europe (I think). I'm hoping that might improve our collective understanding of it.

Yoav Weiss Noam Rosenthal: Btw, the reason I kept "[traversable navigable](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/document-sequences.html#traversable-navigables)" and "top-level traversable" as separate things is because of your use-case, and the general idea of having an iframe that doesn't take part in top-level traversal.
content.formatted_body: Yoav Weiss Noam Rosenthal: Btw, the reason I kept "traversable navigable" and "top-level traversable" as separate things is because of your use-case, and the general idea of having an iframe that doesn't take part in top-level traversal.

content.body: D makes some sense to me. When we added the source IDL attribute it already seemed a bit of a hack that it wasn’t integrated with event dispatch like relatedTarget.
D is like the current relatedTarget approach, no?  mouseover/out/focus/focusin/focusout/blur which have .relatedTarget are also composed
Is that not the behaviour that was removed in https://github.com/whatwg/html/pull/11255? Am I misunderstanding the proposal?

Alice (🇳🇱): I think it is, so it might need some adjusting. One could imagine doing a check when dispatching as to whether the event should be composed or not based on where source is relative to target. The core concern I have with A is that it changes get the parent.
content.formatted_body: Alice (🇳🇱): I think it is, so it might need some adjusting. One could imagine doing a check when dispatching as to whether the event should be composed or not based on where source is relative to target. The core concern I have with A is that it changes get the parent.

content.body: The goal of that was to honour the intent of not having `composed` set, while still allowing listeners in `source`'s tree to observe the event
content.formatted_body: The goal of that was to honour the intent of not having composed set, while still allowing listeners in source's tree to observe the event

content.body: It seems like a big hammer to set `composed` just to get the event to propagate into `source`'s tree
content.formatted_body: It seems like a big hammer to set composed just to get the event to propagate into source's tree

content.body: but I don't really have a horse in this race, so if folks are ok with it after all then so am I

I think if coupled with relatedTarget-like semantics it ends up being mostly equivalent, except that intermediate shadow roots are no longer skipped. Because it's also somewhat weird for non-composed events to nevertheless escape the shadow root. That's pretty magical too.
I guess I'm glad we hadn't done multiple shadow roots per element as that would make all of this even more mind boggling.
* I guess I'm glad we haven't done multiple shadow roots per element as that would make all of this even more mind boggling.

m.new_content.body: I guess I'm glad we haven't done multiple shadow roots per element as that would make all of this even more mind boggling.

content.body: I need to refresh my memory on the relatedTarget behaviour and the intent behind it
I don't really know what people mean when they describe behaviour as magical; I always just read it as "bad vibes"
Bad vibes sounds accurate. :-) I think what I usually mean is that the behavior seems unexpected given the shape of whatever is being discussed. Perhaps to a severe degree.
IMO magic is when something pulls tricks to achieve the desired outcome, and those tricks are usually not exposed as their own lower-level primitives. e.g. `fieldset` + `legend` rendering is magic
content.formatted_body: IMO magic is when something pulls tricks to achieve the desired outcome, and those tricks are usually not exposed as their own lower-level primitives. e.g. fieldset + legend rendering is magic

content.body: It is not. Those events were just composed, but .source handling didn't trigger similar behavior as .relatedTarget
Or I misinterpret what claude means in D
